notes: This meeting is a post-mortem on a failed feature (annotation/Mini ACE sub-agent). It reveals that the team was NOT converging during the feature's development and the retro itself only partially converges on what went wrong. Sam drives the forensic walkthrough almost entirely alone, asking structured questions. The team provides honest answers, but the responses expose fragmentation, siloed communication, suppressed concerns, and absence of shared design understanding. The meeting does not reach convergence on remediation — Sam explicitly defers solutions ('we're not going to do that today') and instead closes with an aspirational statement that Yumi will fix the design gap. The team aligns on the factual record of failure but does not converge on a shared plan for what to change. | Caveats: A retrospective is structurally designed to examine failure, so a 'not_converging' judgment partly reflects the meeting's genre rather than pure group dynamics. However, retros CAN converge — on shared understanding of root causes and on remediation commitments. This one converges weakly on facts but not on remediation.; Sam's facilitator role creates asymmetric power dynamics. His monologues (cavitation, unbounded work intake) are received in silence, which could indicate either absorption or disengagement. Without prosodic cues, text analysis alone cannot distinguish these.; The meeting is a precursor to the March 10 onsite sessions (Transcripts 001 and 002). Many of the concepts Sam introduces here (red cord, design gate, batch size) are picked up and contested productively four days later. So the non-convergence in this meeting may have been necessary for the convergence that follows.; TJ and Vladi's admissions of suppressed concern are high-trust disclosures. In a healthier reading, this IS convergence — the team is being honest about what went wrong. The 'not_converging' judgment reflects that this honesty does not lead to shared remediation in this session, not that the honesty itself is bad.
